Arkham Horror: The Card Game.name: Arkham Horror LCG
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Arkham Horror: The Card Game.description: Build a deck, become an investigator, and descend into Lovecraftian nightmares where every scenario rewrites reality around you.
Arkham Horror: The Card Game.justification: M(4): Deck construction with multi-use cards and cross-class synergies. T(5): Investigating mythos horrors IS the mechanic — chaos bag creates dread. G(4): Every card play and movement choice carries narrative weight. F(2): Deckbuilding between scenarios and token management add overhead. Ar(5): Evocative Lovecraftian art across hundreds of unique cards.

Arkham Horror: The Card Game (Revised Co.name: AH:LCG (Revised Core)
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-05 Hidden and Exposed Victory Points
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Arkham Horror: The Card Game (Revised Co.description: The definitive entry point into Arkham's nightmares — streamlined components, same soul-crushing mythos.
Arkham Horror: The Card Game (Revised Co.justification: M(4): Same deep deck construction engine with tighter card pool. T(5): Chaos bag and encounter deck still generate pure investigative dread. G(4): Meaningful deckbuilding and scenario choices persist. F(2): Revised layout helps but inherent complexity remains. Ar(5): Updated card formatting with the same hauntingly beautiful illustrations.

Burgle Bros..name: Burgle Bros.
Burgle Bros..description: Assemble a crew, crack a multi-story building's safes, and escape the roof before patrolling guards catch you.
Burgle Bros..justification: M(3): Tile-flip exploration with action point management. T(4): Sneaking past guards and cracking safes IS how you play — tension is procedural. G(3): Meaningful route planning but limited options per turn. F(1): Tiny box, fast setup, clean rules. Ar(4): Tim Foley's retro heist art nails the caper aesthetic.

Cards Against Humanity.name: Cards Against Humanity
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Cards Against Humanity.description: Fill in the blanks with the most offensive thing you can find and let your friends judge your depravity.
Cards Against Humanity.justification: M(1): Play a card from your hand. That's it. T(1): No thematic experience beyond shock value. G(1): Random hand, subjective judging, no meaningful decisions. F(1): Open box and play immediately. Ar(1): Black and white text on cards — functional at best.

Cheating Moth.name: Cheating Moth
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Cheating Moth.description: A card-shedding game where cheating is not only allowed but required — just don't let the bug-eyed guard catch you.
Cheating Moth.justification: M(1): Basic card matching with a single gimmick. T(2): The cheating mechanic adds a mischievous social layer. G(1): Limited decisions beyond when to sneak a card away. F(1): Tiny card game, instant setup. Ar(3): Charming, colorful insect illustrations.

Cyberion.name: Cyberion
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Cyberion.description: Salvage cybernetic parts from a dying space station, combining them into powerful combos before the system shuts down forever.
Cyberion.justification: M(3): Multi-use cards with set collection and satisfying combo chains. T(3): Scavenging and upgrading feels thematic but abstracted. G(3): Tight hand management with meaningful exchange decisions. F(1): Small footprint, fast to teach and play. Ar(4): Slick cyberpunk aesthetic with excellent card design.

Deep Sea Adventure.name: Deep Sea Adventure
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Deep Sea Adventure.description: Divers share one oxygen tank and greedily grab treasure from the ocean floor — but everyone breathing from the same supply means someone's drowning.
Deep Sea Adventure.justification: M(2): Roll, move, grab — push-your-luck at its simplest. T(3): Shared oxygen creates genuine social tension and greed. G(2): Limited to stay-or-go decisions with some timing. F(1): Fits in a pocket, plays in 20 minutes. Ar(3): Minimalist Oink Games design, clean and functional.

Final Girl.name: Final Girl
Final Girl.description: You are the last survivor in a slasher film — fight the killer, save victims, and survive the night using only your wits and a dwindling action economy.
Final Girl.justification: M(4): Action points, stat checks, and probability management interlock tightly. T(5): Every mechanic generates horror movie moments — you ARE the final girl. G(4): Constant triage between saving victims, fighting, and fleeing. F(2): Setup with multiple decks and boards takes time. Ar(5): Each feature film box has stunning, genre-authentic artwork.

Gloomhaven: Jaws of the Lion.name: Gloomhaven: JotL
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Gloomhaven: Jaws of the Lion.description: Choose two cards, commit to your plan, and watch it collide with the dungeon's chaos in a campaign that permanently evolves your mercenary.
Gloomhaven: Jaws of the Lion.justification: M(4): Dual-card action selection with exhaustion creates deep hand management. T(4): Card loss as stamina and unlocking abilities generates dungeon-crawl tension. G(4): Simultaneous selection means committing before seeing outcomes. F(2): Scenario setup and rules density require investment. Ar(4): Strong map tiles and character art with clear iconography.

Harmonies.name: Harmonies
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
Harmonies.description: Draft colored tokens and stack them into landscapes, building habitats that attract animals to your personal nature tableau.
Harmonies.justification: M(3): Drafting and spatial stacking with pattern matching. T(2): Nature theme is pleasant but mechanics don't generate it. G(3): Meaningful draft choices and placement optimization. F(1): Simple rules, fast setup, elegant flow. Ar(4): Beautiful pastel palette and calming visual design.

Hotel Lovecraft.name: Hotel Lovecraft
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Hotel Lovecraft.description: Explore the rooms of a Lovecraftian hotel where cosmic horrors lurk behind every door and sanity is the real currency.
Hotel Lovecraft.justification: M(2): Basic action points with stat checks and unknown information. T(3): Lovecraftian setting provides atmosphere but mechanics are conventional. G(2): Choices feel limited by dice and card draws. F(2): Multiple decks and tokens to manage. Ar(3): Adequate horror theming but nothing standout.

In Vino Morte.name: In Vino Morte
In Vino Morte.description: Poison your rivals' wine while trying not to drink from the wrong glass yourself.
In Vino Morte.justification: M(1): Simple push-your-luck bluffing with minimal structure. T(2): Poisoning theme adds flavor but mechanics are bare. G(1): Almost entirely luck-driven with hidden information. F(1): A handful of cards, plays instantly. Ar(1): Minimal production value.

Moon Adventure.name: Moon Adventure
Moon Adventure.description: Manage your oxygen and supplies across the lunar surface, pushing deeper into unexplored territory before your resources run dry.
Moon Adventure.justification: M(2): Push-your-luck with multi-use cards and basic action points. T(3): Shared oxygen and exploration evoke lunar survival tension. G(2): Decisions feel constrained by card draws. F(1): Compact Oink Games format, quick setup. Ar(3): Clean, minimal space aesthetic.

Nemesis.name: Nemesis
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Nemesis.description: Wake up from hypersleep, discover aliens have infested your ship, and try to survive — but everyone has their own secret agenda.
Nemesis.justification: M(3): Action points with map exploration and hidden objectives. T(5): Every noise roll, room reveal, and contamination check IS the alien horror film. G(4): Meaningful decisions about fighting, fleeing, and trusting crewmates. F(4): Massive setup with unpainted miniatures that required commissioning a painter. Ar(4): Incredible alien and ship design, docked for out-of-box unfinished miniatures.

Paleo.name: Paleo
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Paleo.description: Lead a prehistoric tribe through a harsh world where every card flip could mean food, danger, or the discovery that changes your people's future.
Paleo.justification: M(3): Unknown information and multi-use cards with cooperative resource pooling. T(4): Card-back scouting and communal survival generate tribal narrative. G(3): Meaningful choices about which dangers to face and resources to share. F(2): Multiple module decks and setup for each scenario. Ar(4): Warm, evocative prehistoric illustrations.

Rafter Five.name: Rafter Five
Rafter Five.description: Race wooden rafts down a river, collecting sets of cargo before your opponents reach the finish line.
Rafter Five.justification: M(2): Simple set collection and movement. T(2): River racing theme is present but mechanically thin. G(2): Basic tactical choices about movement and collection. F(1): Fast-playing filler with minimal rules. Ar(3): Pleasant, colorful component design.

Sentinels of the Multiverse: Definitive.name: Sentinels of the Multiverse DE
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Sentinels of the Multiverse: Definitive.description: Each hero is a living comic book — play your unique deck of powers against escalating supervillains in battles that tell stories through card combos.
Sentinels of the Multiverse: Definitive.justification: M(4): Variable powers with tag-based combos and synergistic card chains. T(4): Each hero deck generates a unique comic-book power fantasy. G(4): Strategic sequencing of powers and team coordination. F(2): Managing multiple villain and environment decks takes effort. Ar(4): Bold comic-book art style with strong character identities.

Skulls of Sedlec.name: Skulls of Sedlec
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Skulls of Sedlec.description: Arrange exhumed skulls into a beautiful ossuary display, scoring points for how elegantly the dead are stacked.
Skulls of Sedlec.justification: M(2): Simple tile-laying with adjacency-based set scoring. T(2): Bone-stacking theme is morbidly charming but abstract. G(2): Placement choices matter but options are limited. F(1): Eighteen cards, plays in ten minutes. Ar(3): Delightfully macabre skull illustrations.

Sky Team.name: Sky Team
Sky Team.description: You and your co-pilot must land a plane in total silence, communicating only through dice placement.
Sky Team.justification: M(3): Dice-as-workers placed across cockpit systems with tight tolerances. T(4): The silence rule doesn't describe pressure — it creates it. G(3): Every die placement is a meaningful gamble on your partner's intent. F(1): Small box, clean rules, fast setup. Ar(5): Gorgeous cockpit board and destination art by Eric Hibbeler.

Spirit Island.name: Spirit Island
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Spirit Island.description: Ancient spirits unite to drive colonizing invaders from their sacred island through elemental fury and growing power.
Spirit Island.justification: M(5): Interlocking spirit powers, elements, and innate abilities create deep combos. T(5): You ARE the spirit — every power generates the elemental defense fantasy. G(5): Enormous decision tree with cascading consequences across the island. F(3): Setup and upkeep manageable but rules density is high. Ar(5): Stunning spirit art and lush island board by Jason Behnke.

Sprawlopolis.name: Sprawlopolis
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
Sprawlopolis.description: Eighteen cards, infinite city — lay roads, parks, and districts to build a metropolis that scores against its own shifting victory conditions.
Sprawlopolis.justification: M(3): Multi-use cards as both scoring rules and city tiles. T(2): City-building theme exists but decisions feel spatial, not narrative. G(3): Every placement has cascading scoring implications. F(1): Fits in a wallet, plays in fifteen minutes. Ar(3): Functional, colorful city-block illustrations.

The Bloody Inn.name: The Bloody Inn
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
The Bloody Inn.description: Run a quaint French inn where you murder wealthy guests and bury them in your garden for profit.
The Bloody Inn.justification: M(3): Multi-use cards with tight hand management and action economy. T(4): Murdering, bribing, and burying guests IS the mechanic — darkly thematic. G(3): Meaningful choices each round but limited options keep it focused. F(1): Teach in five minutes, play in thirty. Ar(5): Weberson Santiago's macabre illustrations are iconic.

The Crew: Mission Deep Sea.name: The Crew: Mission Deep Sea
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
The Crew: Mission Deep Sea.description: A cooperative trick-taking game where you must win specific tricks in specific orders — and you can barely communicate how.
The Crew: Mission Deep Sea.justification: M(3): Trick-taking with cooperative constraints and mission targets. T(3): Communication limits generate tension but the undersea theme is light. G(3): Playing the right card at the right time is genuinely strategic. F(1): Standard card game, fast setup, simple rules. Ar(3): Clean nautical design, functional but not striking.

The Last Lighthouse.name: The Last Lighthouse
The Last Lighthouse.description: Keep your lighthouse burning on a sinking island, laying tiles to hold back the rising sea while managing dwindling resources.
The Last Lighthouse.justification: M(3): Tile-laying with action management and probability mitigation. T(3): The sinking island creates escalating tension and isolation. G(3): Deciding where to build and which threats to address feels meaningful. F(1): Small box solo game, fast to set up. Ar(4): Atmospheric coastal art with a somber, solitary mood.

Tokyo Highway.name: Tokyo Highway
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Tokyo Highway.description: Build an impossible tangle of elevated highways with tweezers and steady hands, threading roads over and under your opponents' structures.
Tokyo Highway.justification: M(2): Dexterity-based network building with simple rules. T(2): Highway construction theme is present but secondary to dexterity. G(2): Placement matters but physical skill dominates strategy. F(1): Minimal components, straightforward setup. Ar(4): Stunning minimalist aesthetic — the table becomes the art.

Trails.name: Trails
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Trails.description: Hike along a national park trail, collecting resources and photographing wildlife as the sun arcs across the sky.
Trails.justification: M(2): Basic resource exchange and set collection along a linear path. T(2): Park theme is pleasant but mechanics don't evoke hiking. G(2): Limited decisions about when to turn around on the trail. F(1): Light rules, quick setup, short playtime. Ar(3): Pleasant nature illustrations in the Parks series style.

Under Falling Skies.name: Under Falling Skies
Under Falling Skies.description: Alone in a bunker beneath an alien mothership, you roll dice and assign them to defend your city — but higher numbers bring the invaders closer.
Under Falling Skies.justification: M(4): Dice-as-workers with a brilliant risk-reward tension on every placement. T(4): Higher dice = better actions but faster alien descent creates genuine dread. G(4): Every die placement forces agonizing trade-offs between offense and defense. F(1): Solo game in a small box with elegant, fast setup. Ar(3): Functional sci-fi art that serves the game without dazzling.

Unsurmountable.name: Unsurmountable
Unsurmountable.description: Lay path tiles up a treacherous mountain, pushing your luck against avalanches and dead ends in a solo climbing puzzle.
Unsurmountable.justification: M(2): Simple tile-laying with push-your-luck elements. T(2): Mountain climbing theme is present but abstract. G(2): Placement decisions matter but randomness dominates. F(1): Tiny solo game, instant setup. Ar(3): Clean, pleasant mountain-themed art.

Warp's Edge.name: Warp's Edge
Warp's Edge.description: A lone starfighter pilot trapped in a time loop, replaying the same battle with an ever-evolving bag of tokens until you finally break through.
Warp's Edge.justification: M(4): Bag-building with rerolling, locking, and upgrade paths across loops. T(4): The time-loop mechanic IS the narrative — each warp teaches you something new. G(4): Meaningful decisions about which tokens to add, keep, or spend. F(1): Solo bag-builder with fast, clean setup. Ar(4): Vibrant sci-fi art with distinct boss and ship designs.

Earthborne Rangers.name: Earthborne Rangers
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Earthborne Rangers.description: Wander a rewilded future Earth as a ranger, building your path through a living open world that remembers every choice you make.
Earthborne Rangers.justification: M(4): Deck construction with multi-use cards and branching narrative paths. T(5): The open-world structure makes you feel like you inhabit this future ecology. G(4): Freeform exploration with genuine narrative consequences. F(3): Campaign tracking and large card pool require commitment. Ar(5): Breathtaking solarpunk landscapes and character art throughout.

Arkham Horror: The Card Game – Murder at.name: AH:LCG – Murder at the Excelsior Hotel
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – Murder at.description: Wake up in a luxury hotel surrounded by corpses — now prove your innocence while eldritch forces close in around you.
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – Murder at.justification: M(4): Same deep deck construction engine in a standalone murder mystery. T(5): Whodunit structure layered over mythos horror creates unique tension. G(4): Investigation choices branch the scenario in dramatically different directions. F(2): Requires core set and deckbuilding preparation. Ar(5): Gorgeous hotel setting with classic Arkham Horror illustration quality.

Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Path.name: AH:LCG – Path to Carcosa: Campaign
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Path.description: Attend a performance of The King in Yellow and watch reality unravel as madness becomes the campaign's central mechanic.
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Path.justification: M(4): Core deck construction engine with new doubt/conviction mechanics. T(5): Sanity and unreliable reality are mechanized — you question your own campaign state. G(4): Choices about embracing or fighting madness shape the entire campaign. F(2): Full campaign requiring core set and substantial deckbuilding. Ar(5): Haunting Carcosa imagery with the series' best atmospheric art.

Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Path.name: AH:LCG – Path to Carcosa: Investigator
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Path.description: New investigators shaped by madness — artists, mystics, and performers whose powers blur the line between brilliance and insanity.
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Path.justification: M(4): Fresh investigator decks with unique madness-adjacent abilities. T(5): Each investigator's mechanics reflect their relationship to the Yellow Sign. G(4): New deckbuilding options and investigator-specific strategies. F(2): Requires core set and campaign expansion for full use. Ar(5): Character portraits and card art capture the Carcosa cycle's unsettling beauty.

Cards Against Humanity: The A.I. Pack.name: CAH: The A.I. Pack
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Cards Against Humanity: The A.I. Pack.description: The same crude fill-in-the-blank game, now with cards about robots, algorithms, and the singularity.
Cards Against Humanity: The A.I. Pack.justification: M(1): Identical zero-decision mechanic as the base game. T(1): AI theme is cosmetic text on cards. G(1): No meaningful choices — just play what seems funniest. F(1): Shuffle into existing deck and go. Ar(1): Same black-and-white text-only cards.

Final Girl: Hell to Pay.name: Final Girl: Hell to Pay
Final Girl: Hell to Pay.description: Survive a nightclub overtaken by demonic forces, where the dance floor becomes a killing ground and every exit leads deeper into hell.
Final Girl: Hell to Pay.justification: M(4): Core Final Girl engine with location-specific modular mechanics. T(5): Demonic nightclub setting generates unique horror scenarios through gameplay. G(4): Triage decisions intensified by the venue's tight, dangerous layout. F(2): Feature film setup with multiple decks and location boards. Ar(5): Infernal nightclub art dripping with horror-movie atmosphere.

Final Girl: Into the Void.name: Final Girl: Into the Void
Final Girl: Into the Void.description: Trapped on a space station with an alien predator, you must survive in zero-gravity corridors where every airlock is both weapon and death trap.
Final Girl: Into the Void.justification: M(4): Core engine enhanced with zero-gravity and airlock-specific mechanics. T(5): Sci-fi horror isolation is generated by the station's spatial mechanics. G(4): Airlock gambles and vacuum exposure add unique tactical layers. F(2): Feature film setup with station-specific components. Ar(5): Cold, clinical space station art that channels Alien perfectly.

Final Girl: Madness in the Dark.name: Final Girl: Madness in the Dark
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Final Girl: Madness in the Dark.description: Navigate a pitch-black asylum where the darkness itself is a mechanic — you can't fight what you can't see.
Final Girl: Madness in the Dark.justification: M(4): Core engine with darkness and visibility as additional mechanical layers. T(5): Darkness mechanics generate fear — limited vision IS the horror. G(4): Flashlight management and blind movement create agonizing choices. F(2): Feature film setup plus darkness overlay components. Ar(5): Oppressive asylum art that makes the board feel claustrophobic.

Final Girl: Panic at Station 2891.name: Final Girl: Panic at Station 2891
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Final Girl: Panic at Station 2891.description: An arctic research station, a shape-shifting alien, and no way to know if the person you just saved is still human.
Final Girl: Panic at Station 2891.justification: M(4): Core engine with paranoia and contamination mechanics. T(5): Trust-no-one paranoia is mechanized — rescued victims might be the threat. G(4): Saving people who might betray you creates unique decision tension. F(2): Feature film setup with paranoia-specific components. Ar(5): Frozen wasteland art channeling The Thing with gorgeous isolation.

The Bloody Inn: The Carnies.name: The Bloody Inn: The Carnies
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
The Bloody Inn: The Carnies.description: The travelling carnival has come to your murderous inn — and the new guests bring tricks, illusions, and even darker opportunities.
The Bloody Inn: The Carnies.justification: M(3): Same multi-use card engine with new carnival guest abilities. T(4): Carnival performers add macabre variety to the murder-for-profit loop. G(3): New guest powers expand tactical options within the tight framework. F(1): Same fast teach and compact footprint as the base. Ar(5): Santiago's carnival characters match the base game's iconic style.

The Last Lighthouse: Expansion Collectio.name: The Last Lighthouse: Expansion Collection
The Last Lighthouse: Expansion Collectio.description: New threats, new tools, and new ways for your lighthouse island to crumble into the sea.
The Last Lighthouse: Expansion Collectio.justification: M(3): Expanded tile and event options add variety to the core engine. T(3): New modules deepen the isolation and escalation. G(3): Additional decision points from new threat types and tools. F(1): Modular expansions that slot cleanly into the base game. Ar(4): Maintains the base game's atmospheric coastal aesthetic.

Mage Knight Board Game.name: Mage Knight
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Mage Knight Board Game.description: Command a powerful Mage Knight exploring a vast fantasy landscape, conquering cities and leveling up through one of gaming's deepest solo puzzles.
Mage Knight Board Game.justification: M(5): Deck construction, grid movement, and multi-system upgrades create immense depth. T(3): Fantasy adventure theme present but mechanics feel like a puzzle, not a story. G(5): Every hand is a complex optimization problem with real consequences. F(5): Notoriously heavy setup, rules overhead, and lengthy play sessions. Ar(3): Functional fantasy art that serves without inspiring.

ROVE: Results-Oriented Versatile Explore.name: ROVE
ROVE: Results-Oriented Versatile Explore.description: Guide a tiny Mars rover across shifting terrain cards, solving spatial puzzles one mission at a time.
ROVE: Results-Oriented Versatile Explore.justification: M(2): Simple tile-sliding with mission-based objectives. T(2): Mars rover theme is referenced but not generated by mechanics. G(3): Meaningful spatial puzzle decisions despite small footprint. F(1): Wallet-sized solo game, instant setup. Ar(3): Clean, functional space-themed card art.

SETI: Search for Extraterrestrial Intell.name: SETI
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
SETI: Search for Extraterrestrial Intell.description: Scan the cosmos for alien signals, launching probes and building research networks in a race to make first contact.
SETI: Search for Extraterrestrial Intell.justification: M(4): Worker placement with income engines, set collection, and gating. T(3): Space exploration theme is present but mechanics lean euro. G(4): Meaningful worker and resource allocation decisions. F(3): Multiple interconnected boards and resource tracks. Ar(4): Elegant space-science aesthetic with detailed probe and signal art.

Speakeasy.name: Speakeasy
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Speakeasy.description: Run a Prohibition-era empire of illegal bars, bribing officials, muscling rivals, and keeping your operation hidden from the feds.
Speakeasy.justification: M(4): Worker placement with area control and hidden information. T(4): Bribery, territory, and secrecy mechanics generate the gangster fantasy. G(4): Balancing expansion with concealment creates tense strategic decisions. F(3): Multiple boards, hidden information tracking, and complex interactions. Ar(4): Atmospheric 1920s noir visual design.

Anachrony.name: Anachrony
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Anachrony.description: Lead a faction through a time-bending apocalypse, borrowing resources from your own future and hoping you can pay them back before paradox destroys everything.
Anachrony.justification: M(5): Worker placement with time travel, asymmetric factions, and upgrade paths. T(4): Borrowing from the future and creating time paradoxes IS the core mechanic. G(4): Complex multi-turn planning with temporal debt consequences. F(4): Exosuit miniatures, multiple boards, and time-travel tracking overhead. Ar(4): Striking post-apocalyptic sci-fi aesthetic and faction design.

Arkham Noir: Case #1 – The Witch Cult Mu.name: Arkham Noir #1
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Arkham Noir: Case #1 – The Witch Cult Mu.description: A solo noir investigation dealt in cards — connect clues into case files before time runs out and the cult completes its ritual.
Arkham Noir: Case #1 – The Witch Cult Mu.justification: M(3): Hand management with set collection and narrative branching. T(4): Card connections generate the noir investigation experience procedurally. G(3): Meaningful choices about which leads to pursue and when to close cases. F(1): Small card game with fast setup. Ar(4): Black-and-white noir illustrations ooze pulp detective atmosphere.

Burgle Bros 2: The Casino Capers.name: Burgle Bros 2
Burgle Bros 2: The Casino Capers.description: Your heist crew infiltrates a Vegas casino — but now the guards are smarter, the floors are flashier, and you need disguises to blend in.
Burgle Bros 2: The Casino Capers.justification: M(3): Evolved tile-flip exploration with disguise and escalation mechanics. T(4): Disguise management and casino infiltration generate caper-film tension. G(3): Route planning with new stealth variables adds depth. F(2): Larger footprint and more components than the original. Ar(4): Upgraded retro-Vegas art style expands Tim Foley's heist world.

Coffee Roaster.name: Coffee Roaster
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Coffee Roaster.description: Draw beans from a bag, roast them to perfection, and serve the ideal cup — a solo bag-management puzzle wrapped in coffee culture.
Coffee Roaster.justification: M(3): Bag-building with probability management and set evaluation. T(2): Coffee theme is charming but mechanics feel abstract. G(3): Meaningful push-your-luck and bean management decisions. F(1): Small solo game with minimal setup. Ar(3): Warm, pleasant coffee-themed illustrations.

Cthulhu: Death May Die.name: Cthulhu: Death May Die
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Cthulhu: Death May Die.description: You're not trying to prevent the ritual — it's too late for that. Instead, drive yourself mad enough to fight an Elder God face to face.
Cthulhu: Death May Die.justification: M(3): Dice rolling with action points and character progression. T(4): Embracing madness to gain power generates Lovecraftian horror brilliantly. G(3): Balancing sanity loss against power gains creates real tension. F(3): Large miniatures with multiple boards and scenario setup. Ar(4): Impressive Elder God sculpts and dark, atmospheric board art.

Daybreak.name: Daybreak
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Daybreak.description: Cooperatively avert climate catastrophe by deploying clean energy, passing policies, and managing cascading environmental crises across four world powers.
Daybreak.justification: M(3): Simultaneous action selection with resource exchange and crisis management. T(3): Climate theme is earnest but mechanics feel system-management focused. G(3): Coordination decisions and resource allocation carry weight. F(2): Multiple player boards and crisis tracking. Ar(4): Matt Leacock's clean, modern design with striking environmental art.

EXIT: The Game – The Abandoned Cabin.name: EXIT: Abandoned Cabin
EXIT: The Game – The Abandoned Cabin.description: Locked in a cabin with only cryptic clues and a decoder wheel — solve the puzzles or never leave.
EXIT: The Game – The Abandoned Cabin.justification: M(2): Puzzle-solving with a decoder mechanism and narrative clue chains. T(3): Being trapped and solving to escape generates genuine urgency. G(3): Lateral thinking puzzles with meaningful eureka moments. F(2): One-time play and some components require destruction. Ar(3): Adequate cabin setting with functional puzzle presentation.

Gaia Project.name: Gaia Project
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
Gaia Project.description: Fourteen alien species terraform planets across a modular galaxy, each evolving along unique technology tracks in a deep strategic competition.
Gaia Project.justification: M(5): Network building with asymmetric factions, income engines, and upgrade tech. T(2): Sci-fi theme is thin — this is a pure euro optimization puzzle. G(5): Every action has cascading strategic implications across multiple systems. F(3): Complex setup with many tracks, tokens, and round scoring tiles. Ar(3): Functional space aesthetic — clean but not visually striking.

Horrified: World of Monsters.name: Horrified: World of Monsters
Horrified: World of Monsters.description: Classic Universal Monsters stalk a village and only your team of townsfolk can solve each creature's unique weakness before it's too late.
Horrified: World of Monsters.justification: M(2): Simple action points with grid movement and item collection. T(3): Each monster's unique defeat condition evokes its film origin. G(2): Decisions are straightforward with limited strategic depth. F(2): Multiple monster setups and village board management. Ar(4): Beautiful Universal Monsters art with vibrant, atmospheric design.

Imperium: Horizons.name: Imperium: Horizons
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Imperium: Horizons.description: Guide a civilization from barbarism to empire through a deck that literally evolves — your cards change as your people advance through history.
Imperium: Horizons.justification: M(4): Deckbuilding with asymmetric civilizations, income, and state transitions. T(3): Civilization growth is mechanized but feels abstract at card level. G(4): When to trigger your civilization's evolution is deeply strategic. F(3): Dense rulebook with civilization-specific exceptions and interactions. Ar(4): Rich, diverse civilization art across dozens of unique cultures.

It's a Wonderful World.name: It's a Wonderful World
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
It's a Wonderful World.description: Draft blueprint cards, scrap what you don't need for resources, and engine-build a dystopian empire one production chain at a time.
It's a Wonderful World.justification: M(3): Card drafting with combo-driven engine building. T(2): Dystopian theme is cosmetic — this is pure engine optimization. G(3): Draft and commit decisions create meaningful strategic tension. F(1): Clean rules, fast rounds, minimal setup. Ar(3): Serviceable retro-futurist card illustrations.

Kanban EV.name: Kanban EV
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Kanban EV.description: Work in an electric car factory under a demanding manager, optimizing your production line across departments while competing for employee of the week.
Kanban EV.justification: M(5): Worker placement with income, upgrades, and tightly interlocked departments. T(3): Factory management theme is present but deeply abstracted. G(4): Sandra the manager creates time pressure on strategic planning. F(3): Multiple interconnected boards and complex phase structure. Ar(3): Functional industrial design — clean but utilitarian.

The LOOP.name: The LOOP
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
The LOOP.description: Travel through time to stop a mad scientist's clones from unraveling history, looping your actions to pull off impossible combos.
The LOOP.justification: M(3): Action points with variable powers and campaign progression. T(3): Time-loop theme provides flavor but mechanics are standard co-op. G(3): Card combo potential and clone management offer real decisions. F(2): Multiple boards and clone tracking components. Ar(4): Vibrant, cartoonish time-travel art with strong visual identity.

Nemesis: Retaliation.name: Nemesis: Retaliation
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Nemesis: Retaliation.description: The aliens are back — a new ship, new crew, and new nightmares in deep space where survival means trusting no one.
Nemesis: Retaliation.justification: M(3): Same core action and exploration engine with new alien behaviors. T(5): Fresh alien types and ship layout regenerate the survival horror experience. G(4): New crew abilities and alien mechanics demand adapted strategies. F(4): Same massive setup with unpainted miniatures as the original. Ar(4): New alien designs are terrifying but miniatures arrive unpainted.

On Mars.name: On Mars
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
On Mars.description: Build humanity's first Martian colony by shuttling between orbit and surface, managing life support, and constructing the infrastructure of a new world.
On Mars.justification: M(5): Worker placement with network building, income, and orbital logistics. T(4): Orbit-to-surface duality and life support generate colonization tension. G(4): Dual-location decisions and building placement are deeply strategic. F(4): Massive board, many systems, and Lacerda-level rules density. Ar(5): Ian O'Toole's gorgeous Mars landscape and clean iconography.

Paperback: 10th Anniversary Edition.name: Paperback 10th Ann.
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Paperback: 10th Anniversary Edition.description: Spell words to defeat genre-fiction villains, building your deck of letters into an ever-more-powerful vocabulary engine.
Paperback: 10th Anniversary Edition.justification: M(3): Deck construction meeting word-game mechanics with set scoring. T(3): Pulp fiction framing adds charm — spelling IS your weapon. G(3): Word choice, card purchase, and timing create layered decisions. F(1): Standard card game setup, fast and accessible. Ar(3): Pleasant pulp-novel cover art on card backs.

Regicide.name: Regicide
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Regicide.description: Defeat twelve royals using only a standard deck of cards — suits are your weapons, and every play must count.
Regicide.justification: M(2): Hand management with suit-based combo powers. T(2): Medieval combat framing is thin over pure card mechanics. G(2): Meaningful card sequencing but limited by hand draws. F(1): Uses a standard poker deck, zero setup. Ar(3): Illustrated court cards with bold, regal design.

Resist!.name: Resist!
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Resist!.description: Lead the Spanish Maquis resistance against Franco's regime, managing a hand of guerrilla fighters in a solo narrative card game.
Resist!.justification: M(2): Hand management with probability mitigation and narrative branching. T(3): Historical resistance theme gives weight to card-play decisions. G(3): Choosing which fighters to deploy and which missions to attempt. F(1): Small card game with minimal setup. Ar(3): Functional wartime illustration style.

ROVE: Expansion Collection.name: ROVE: Expansion Collection
ROVE: Expansion Collection.description: New mission modules and terrain challenges for your intrepid little Mars rover.
ROVE: Expansion Collection.justification: M(2): Expanded tile and mission variety on the same core engine. T(2): Same Mars rover theme — referenced, not generated. G(3): New missions add fresh spatial puzzles and difficulty. F(1): Same wallet-sized format with modular additions. Ar(3): Consistent with the base game's clean space art.

Sleeping Gods.name: Sleeping Gods
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Sleeping Gods.description: Captain a steamship crew lost in a strange world, exploring an atlas of interconnected locations where every choice opens new storylines and closes others.
Sleeping Gods.justification: M(4): Hand management with point-to-point exploration and narrative branching. T(5): The open-world atlas and persistent consequences generate genuine wanderlust. G(4): Every destination choice sacrifices others — exploration has real cost. F(3): Massive campaign with save states, large map, and many decks. Ar(5): Lush, painterly world art across a stunning atlas board.

Trailblazers.name: Trailblazers
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
Trailblazers.description: Draft trail cards and build winding paths through the wilderness, racing to complete hiking, biking, and kayaking routes.
Trailblazers.justification: M(2): Simple card drafting with network-building path connections. T(2): Outdoor theme is pleasant but mechanically decorative. G(3): Drafting and path-routing decisions offer genuine strategic choices. F(1): Clean rules, fast rounds, compact footprint. Ar(3): Attractive nature illustrations with a warm palette.

Unconscious Mind.name: Unconscious Mind
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Unconscious Mind.description: Explore the architecture of the human psyche as a Freudian analyst, placing workers across the layers of a patient's unconscious to unlock buried memories.
Unconscious Mind.justification: M(4): Worker placement with action drafting, income, and combo abilities. T(4): Psychoanalysis mechanics genuinely map to Freudian concepts of the mind. G(4): Layered decision-making across conscious and unconscious spaces. F(3): Multiple boards and psychological-system tracking. Ar(4): Surrealist art style that channels the dreamlike subject matter.

Vantage.name: Vantage
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Vantage.description: Lay landscape tiles to create vistas from multiple viewpoints, scoring based on what you can see from different vantage points.
Vantage.justification: M(3): Tile-laying with area influence and multi-perspective scoring. T(2): Landscape theme is present but perspective scoring is abstract. G(3): Tile placement decisions balance multiple scoring angles. F(1): Clean rules with a focused tile-laying loop. Ar(3): Pleasant landscape art on tiles.

The White Castle.name: The White Castle
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
The White Castle.description: Place dice-workers in Himeji Castle's courtyards, gardens, and towers to gain the Daimyo's favor in feudal Japan.
The White Castle.justification: M(3): Workers-as-dice with action drafting and multi-track scoring. T(2): Japanese castle theme is atmospheric but mechanically thin. G(4): Dice value selection creates deep tactical decisions each round. F(2): Multiple scoring tracks and action areas to manage. Ar(4): Elegant Japanese art with a refined, minimalist board.

Apiary.name: Apiary
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Apiary.description: Command a spacefaring bee civilization, sending worker bees across the galaxy to harvest resources and evolve your hive's capabilities.
Apiary.justification: M(3): Worker placement with upgrade paths and gating mechanics. T(2): Space-bees theme is whimsical but mechanics don't generate it. G(3): Worker strength escalation creates meaningful timing decisions. F(2): Multiple boards and resource tracks. Ar(5): Stunning hymenoptera-meets-space-opera visual design throughout.

Arcs.name: Arcs
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Arcs.description: A galactic saga told through cards — build and betray alliances in a space opera where the campaign remembers everything.
Arcs.justification: M(4): Multi-use cards with area control and asymmetric narrative arcs. T(4): Campaign acts generate emergent political drama through card play. G(4): Card commitment and alliance-breaking create high-stakes decisions. F(3): Campaign tracking with multiple faction-specific components. Ar(4): Kyle Ferrin's distinctive art brings the galactic drama to life.

Arkham Horror: The Card Game – Curse of.name: AH:LCG – Curse of the Rougarou
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – Curse of.description: Hunt a werewolf through the bayou in a standalone scenario where the swamp itself is your deadliest enemy.
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – Curse of.justification: M(4): Core deck construction with bayou-specific location mechanics. T(5): Swamp exploration and lycanthrope pursuit generate visceral horror. G(4): Tracking the Rougarou through shifting bayou locations demands adaptive strategy. F(2): Requires core set and deck preparation. Ar(5): Atmospheric bayou and werewolf art in the Arkham style.

Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Circl.name: AH:LCG – Circle Undone
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Circl.description: Investigate a haunted lodge where a spectral séance sets the stage for a campaign steeped in witchcraft and ghostly terrors.
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Circl.justification: M(4): Core engine with new haunt and spectral mechanics. T(5): Witch trials and ghostly encounters mechanized through encounter design. G(4): Campaign choices between the living and dead shape branching paths. F(2): Full campaign requiring core set investment. Ar(5): Haunted New England imagery with the series' signature atmospheric art.

Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Dream.name: AH:LCG – Dream-Eaters
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Dream.description: Two intertwined campaigns — one waking, one dreaming — where investigators in different realities affect each other's fates.
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Dream.justification: M(4): Core engine with dual-campaign structure linking two parallel storylines. T(5): Dream-world mechanics generate surreal horror distinct from waking scenarios. G(4): Managing two parallel campaigns creates unique strategic layers. F(2): Dual-campaign tracking adds organizational overhead. Ar(5): Dreamlands art shifts the palette into ethereal, alien beauty.

Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Forgo.name: AH:LCG – Forgotten Age
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Forgo.description: Venture deep into uncharted jungles where ancient ruins hide venomous traps, temporal anomalies, and horrors older than civilization.
Arkham Horror: The Card Game – The Forgo.justification: M(4): Core engine with supply mechanics and exploration-specific hazards. T(5): Jungle expedition with supply attrition generates survival-exploration horror. G(4): Supply management and branching exploration paths demand careful planning. F(2): Full campaign with expedition-specific components. Ar(5): Lush jungle ruins and serpent imagery expand the Arkham visual universe.

Barcelona.name: Barcelona
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
Barcelona.description: Shape the modernist expansion of 1800s Barcelona, building Gaudi's masterpieces and filling the Eixample grid with citizens and landmarks.
Barcelona.justification: M(3): Worker placement with network building and citizen management. T(2): City-building theme is present but decisions feel mechanical. G(4): Intersection placement and citizen timing create deep strategic choices. F(2): Multiple boards and scoring conditions to track. Ar(5): Gorgeous modernist art celebrating Barcelona's architectural heritage.

Barcelona: Passeig de Gràcia.name: Barcelona: Passeig de Gràcia
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
Barcelona: Passeig de Gràcia.description: An expansion that extends Barcelona's modernist city-building with the iconic boulevard and its architectural treasures.
Barcelona: Passeig de Gràcia.justification: M(3): Same city-building Euro engine. T(2): Barcelona theme is decorative on optimization mechanics. G(4): Added strategic layers through new building options. F(2): Moderate setup with expansion modules. Ar(5): Same gorgeous production quality as the base game.

Beast.name: Beast
Beast.description: One player is the Beast, stalking villagers through forests and swamps; the others are hunters tracking it through footprints and fear.
Beast.justification: M(3): Asymmetric hidden movement with deduction and area control. T(4): Hunter-vs-beast asymmetry generates genuine predator-prey tension. G(4): Both sides face meaningful decisions about revealing or concealing position. F(2): Asymmetric setup with hidden movement tracking. Ar(4): Dark, atmospheric forest art with a folklore-horror tone.

Endeavor: Deep Sea.name: Endeavor: Deep Sea
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
Endeavor: Deep Sea.description: Lead a deep-sea research organization, deploying submersibles and building research networks across the ocean's most mysterious depths.
Endeavor: Deep Sea.justification: M(3): Action drafting with income engines and network building. T(3): Deep-sea exploration theme adds atmosphere to the euro framework. G(3): Building action variety through research creates cascading decisions. F(2): Multiple tracks and building progression to manage. Ar(5): Stunning oceanic art with a refined, elegant board presence.

Everdell Farshore.name: Everdell Farshore
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Everdell Farshore.description: Build a coastal critter civilization on a distant shore, sending workers to gather resources and playing cards to construct your seaside village.
Everdell Farshore.justification: M(3): Worker placement with hand management and combo-driven tableau. T(3): Woodland creatures building a village is charming but mechanically light. G(3): Timing worker placement against card combos creates strategic depth. F(2): Multiple card types and resource tracks to manage. Ar(5): Breathtaking watercolor-style art with adorable critter character design.

For the Queen.name: For the Queen
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
For the Queen.description: A collaborative storytelling game where prompt cards slowly reveal whether your loyalty to the Queen will hold — or shatter.
For the Queen.justification: M(1): Draw a card, answer a question — no traditional mechanics. T(3): Prompts generate genuine emotional narrative about loyalty and betrayal. G(2): Creative freedom is high but there are no strategic decisions. F(1): Open the box and start talking. Ar(3): Elegant card design with evocative queen portraits.

Galactic Cruise.name: Galactic Cruise
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Galactic Cruise.description: Manage a luxury starship, hiring alien crew, upgrading systems, and keeping passengers happy across a cosmic voyage.
Galactic Cruise.justification: M(4): Worker placement with income engines, upgrades, and combo abilities. T(3): Space cruise management is thematic but mechanically euro. G(4): Crew hiring and system upgrades create meaningful engine-building arcs. F(3): Multiple interconnected boards and resource tracks. Ar(5): Gorgeous retro-futurist space art with vibrant alien character design.

The Gallerist.name: The Gallerist
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
The Gallerist.description: Discover artists, buy and display their work, and manipulate the art market to build the most prestigious gallery in the world.
The Gallerist.justification: M(5): Worker placement with market manipulation, artist development, and gallery curation. T(3): Art-world theme is unusually integrated for a euro but still abstracted. G(4): Every action triggers cascading market and reputation consequences. F(3): Lacerda-level complexity with multiple interconnected systems. Ar(4): Refined gallery aesthetic with clean, elegant board design.

Gloomhaven: Buttons & Bugs.name: GH: Buttons & Bugs
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Gloomhaven: Buttons & Bugs.description: The Gloomhaven tactical card system distilled into a solo pocket game — clear dungeons with a single mercenary and a tiny deck.
Gloomhaven: Buttons & Bugs.justification: M(4): Core dual-card selection preserved in a compressed solo format. T(4): Card exhaustion and ability unlocking still generate dungeon-crawl tension. G(4): Same commitment-before-resolution decisions in a tighter package. F(2): Compact but still requires scenario setup and tracking. Ar(4): Miniaturized Gloomhaven art maintains the series' visual identity.

Heat: Pedal to the Metal.name: Heat
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Heat: Pedal to the Metal.description: Push your engine to the breaking point in a card-driven race where playing heat cards means speed now but mechanical failure later.
Heat: Pedal to the Metal.justification: M(3): Multi-use cards with push-your-luck racing and drafting. T(3): Heat management as engine stress genuinely generates racing tension. G(3): Cornering speed, heat management, and drafting create real decisions. F(1): Straightforward setup with clean, fast gameplay. Ar(4): Dynamic racing art with a retro 1960s Grand Prix aesthetic.

Hegemony: Lead Your Class to Victory.name: Hegemony
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Hegemony: Lead Your Class to Victory.description: Play as the working class, middle class, capitalists, or the state itself in a political simulation where class warfare is the entire game.
Hegemony: Lead Your Class to Victory.justification: M(4): Asymmetric factions with market mechanics and political influence. T(4): Class struggle IS the game — each faction's mechanics embody their real-world position. G(4): Policy voting and market manipulation create genuine political tension. F(4): Massive asymmetric rules overhead with economic simulation complexity. Ar(3): Functional political design that prioritizes information over aesthetics.

Men-Nefer.name: Men-Nefer
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Men-Nefer.description: Build the ancient city of Memphis across millennia, placing workers along the Nile to construct monuments that define Egyptian civilization.
Men-Nefer.justification: M(4): Worker placement with area influence, income, and tile-laying. T(2): Ancient Egyptian theme is atmospheric but mechanically standard euro. G(4): Monument placement and worker timing create interlocking decisions. F(3): Multiple boards, eras, and scoring conditions. Ar(4): Rich Egyptian art with detailed monument and Nile illustrations.

MicroMacro: Crime City.name: MicroMacro
MicroMacro: Crime City.description: A giant city map hides dozens of crimes frozen in time — trace the stories backward through the illustrated chaos to solve each case.
MicroMacro: Crime City.justification: M(1): Pure visual search with narrative connection — no traditional mechanics. T(3): Detective work through visual storytelling generates genuine investigation feel. G(2): Observation-based with satisfying discovery but no strategic decisions. F(1): Unfold the map, pick a case, start looking. Ar(4): Incredibly detailed Where's Waldo-style city illustration.

Nautilion.name: Nautilion
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Nautilion.description: Captain a submarine through a Nautilus-inspired odyssey, rolling dice to recruit crew and outrun pursuing enemies in an Oniverse adventure.
Nautilion.justification: M(2): Dice rolling with probability management and push-your-luck. T(3): Submarine voyage in the Oniverse creates dreamlike nautical atmosphere. G(2): Limited agency — dice dictate options more than player choice. F(1): Small solo game with fast, simple setup. Ar(3): Oniverse's surreal, dreamlike art style.

Onirim (Second Edition).name: Onirim
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Onirim (Second Edition).description: Wander through a labyrinthine dreamworld, playing cards in color sequences to find the doors before the deck runs out and the dream collapses.
Onirim (Second Edition).justification: M(2): Hand management with probability management and set collection. T(2): Dream theme is evocative but card play feels abstract. G(2): Meaningful discard decisions but heavily luck-dependent. F(1): Solo card game with minimal setup. Ar(3): Surreal, pastel dreamscape art throughout.

The Quiet Year.name: The Quiet Year
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
The Quiet Year.description: A community survives the apocalypse for one year — draw cards for seasons, map your settlement, and watch tensions build toward an uncertain future.
The Quiet Year.justification: M(2): Card-driven narrative with map-drawing and collaborative worldbuilding. T(3): Seasonal prompts generate emergent post-apocalyptic stories organically. G(3): Drawing the map and making community decisions feels consequential. F(1): A deck of cards and some paper — nothing else needed. Ar(3): Minimalist design that lets the player-drawn map be the art.

Ra.name: Ra
mechs: AUC-03 Turn Order Until Pass Auction
VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Ra.description: Bid with ancient sun tokens on tiles drawn from the Nile — Egyptian civilization rises and falls with every auction.
Ra.justification: M(3): Auction mechanics with set collection and round-based scoring. T(2): Egyptian theme is pasted on — this is pure bidding strategy. G(3): When to invoke Ra and what to bid creates real tension. F(1): Simple rules, fast rounds, elegant design. Ar(3): Classic Knizia-era Egyptian art — pleasant but dated.

Root.name: Root
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Root.description: Woodland factions — cats, birds, rebels, and vagabonds — wage asymmetric war across a forest where every faction plays by entirely different rules.
Root.justification: M(4): Deeply asymmetric factions with area control and hand management. T(3): Factions embody political archetypes but combat feels mechanical. G(4): Each faction's unique victory path demands different strategic thinking. F(3): Teaching four different rule sets and managing asymmetric interactions. Ar(5): Kyle Ferrin's charming woodland art is instantly iconic.

Slay the Spire: The Board Game.name: Slay the Spire
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Slay the Spire: The Board Game.description: The roguelike deckbuilder made physical — fight through three acts of monsters, build your deck from card rewards, and face the heart of the Spire.
Slay the Spire: The Board Game.justification: M(4): Deck construction with combo abilities, variable powers, and gating. T(4): Roguelike progression and deck evolution generate the video game's tension. G(4): Card reward choices and combat sequencing demand strategic thinking. F(2): Multiple decks, enemy setups, and campaign tracking. Ar(4): Faithful adaptation of the video game's distinctive art style.

Star Wars: Rebellion.name: Star Wars: Rebellion
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Star Wars: Rebellion.description: The Empire hunts for the hidden Rebel base across the galaxy while the Rebels spark hope — an asymmetric war told through missions and fleets.
Star Wars: Rebellion.justification: M(4): Asymmetric grand strategy with hidden base, fleet combat, and missions. T(4): Empire-vs-Rebellion asymmetry generates the Star Wars political-military fantasy. G(4): Hidden base placement and mission assignment create tense cat-and-mouse. F(4): Massive board, many miniatures, and complex combat resolution. Ar(5): Gorgeous Star Wars art with iconic ship miniatures and galaxy board.

Thunder Road: Vendetta.name: Thunder Road
Thunder Road: Vendetta.description: Demolition derby on a desert highway that scrolls endlessly forward — if your car falls off the back edge of the map, it's gone forever.
Thunder Road: Vendetta.justification: M(2): Dice-driven racing with area control and vehicle combat. T(3): Cars exploding and falling off the map generates Road Warrior chaos. G(3): Meaningful decisions about attacking rivals versus racing ahead. F(1): Fast setup with a brilliantly simple scrolling board. Ar(4): Retro 1980s post-apocalyptic art with explosive energy.

5 Towers.name: 5 Towers
mechs: VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
5 Towers.description: Draft numbered cards to build five ascending towers — but every card you take commits you to a column you might regret.
5 Towers.justification: M(2): Simple trick-style drafting with set collection constraints. T(1): Tower theme is purely cosmetic. G(2): When to take and which tower to build offers light decisions. F(1): A handful of cards, plays in fifteen minutes. Ar(2): Minimal, functional card design.

51st State: Ultimate Edition.name: 51st State: UE
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
51st State: Ultimate Edition.description: Rebuild civilization after the apocalypse by raiding, trading, or annexing locations — every card can be used three different ways.
51st State: Ultimate Edition.justification: M(3): Multi-use cards with resource exchange and combo-building. T(3): Post-apocalyptic rebuilding comes through in card flavor and actions. G(3): Triple-use cards create meaningful strategic fork at every play. F(2): Card-heavy setup with multiple resource types. Ar(3): Gritty post-apocalyptic art — functional but not eye-catching.

Bloc by Bloc: Uprising.name: Bloc by Bloc
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Bloc by Bloc: Uprising.description: Coordinate an urban insurrection across a modular city grid — barricade streets, liberate districts, and clash with riot police before the state crushes you.
Bloc by Bloc: Uprising.justification: M(3): Area control with action points and grid-based tactical movement. T(4): Barricading, rioting, and police suppression generate protest-movement tension. G(3): District liberation and police avoidance create meaningful tactical choices. F(2): Modular city board setup with multiple faction components. Ar(3): Stark, political-poster art style — effective but austere.

Café.name: Café
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Café.description: Brew specialty coffees and serve demanding customers in a cozy café where every blend matters.
Café.justification: M(2): Light combo-building with hand management. T(2): Café theme is pleasant but mechanics are generic resource conversion. G(2): Decisions exist but limited strategic depth. F(1): Quick setup, simple rules. Ar(3): Clean, warm art style.

Canvas.name: Canvas
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points from Game State
VIC-02 Victory Points from Player Actions
Canvas.description: Layer transparent cards to compose paintings, combining icons into scoring sets — the art you create is literally the game.
Canvas.justification: M(2): Card drafting with set-based scoring from layered transparency. T(2): Art-creation theme is unique but scoring feels mathematical. CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Fossilium.description: Excavate fossil fragments by drafting cards, assembling prehistoric skeletons for museums in a paleontological set-collection race.
Fossilium.justification: M(2): Card drafting with set collection and assembly. T(2): Fossil excavation theme is present but mechanically simple. G(2): Draft choices matter but depth is limited. F(1): Light card game with fast setup. Ar(3): Charming fossil and dinosaur skeleton illustrations.

The 7th Continent.name: The 7th Continent
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
The 7th Continent.description: Stranded on a cursed continent, explore a vast card-driven wilderness where every terrain tile hides discoveries, dangers, and the slow creep of an ancient doom.
The 7th Continent.justification: M(4): Map addition with multi-use cards, action points, and campaign persistence. T(5): Card-flip exploration generates genuine mystery — the continent reveals itself. G(4): Resource management and exploration routing demand strategic planning. F(3): Massive card-world with save system and lengthy campaign sessions. Ar(5): Lush, hand-painted exploration cards create a breathtaking world.

Container.name: Container
mechs: VIC-01 Victory Points as a Resource
Container.description: An austere economic ballet where every price you set reshapes the entire table's market — pure supply-and-demand abstraction in chunky plastic.
Container.justification: M(3): Interlocking production/shipping/auction system, elegant not complex. T(2): Theme is a frame — you're manipulating prices, not experiencing logistics. G(4): Pricing and auction psychology reshape the market for everyone. F(1): Clean rules, fast setup, elegant flow. Ar(2): Jumbo Edition has nice containers but zero aesthetic atmosphere.

Miskatonic Tales: Journey to Innsmouth.name: Miskatonic Tales: Journey to Innsmouth
mechs: CAR-04 Card Draw, Limits, and Deck Exhaustion
Miskatonic Tales: Journey to Innsmouth.description: A gamebook-driven Lovecraftian investigation where your character deck slowly contaminates with stress and injury — madness is a mechanic, not a flavour text.
Miskatonic Tales: Journey to Innsmouth.justification: M(3): Character decks with push-your-luck stat tests and map exploration, interesting but not deeply interlocking. T(4): Deck contamination generates Lovecraftian deterioration mechanically — CYOA page-flipping occasionally breaks immersion. G(3): Meaningful investigation choices but outcomes often dictated by book and card draws. F(3): Three massive scenario books, map tiles, multiple card types, 6+ hours per adventure — bloated. Ar(3): Chaosium Mythos pedigree, decent illustrations, functional not stunning.
